titlestmt.titleproper: Guide to the Kwame Dawes Papers
titlestmt.author: Michael L. Forstrom

publicationstmt.publisher: Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library

maintenanceagency.agencyname: Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library

descriptivenote.p: Finding aid description and metadata in Archives at Yale are licensed under a CC0 1.0 Universal (CC0 1.0) Public Domain Dedication. This means that anyone may copy, modify, and distribute this descriptive metadata without restriction or permission. The CC0 license is explained in full on the Creative Commons website: CC0 1.0 Universal (CC0 1.0) Public Domain Dedication. More information is available on our Help page.

maintenanceevent.eventdescription: This finding aid was produced using ArchivesSpace on Thursday March 19, 2026 at 23:01

corpname.part: Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library
James S. Jaffe Rare Books LLC

accessrestrict.head: Conditions Governing Access
accessrestrict.p: This collection is open for research.
Boxes 132-137 (audiocassettes): Restricted fragile material. Reference copies may be requested. Consult Access Services for further information.
138 (record album storage): Restricted fragile material. Reference copies may be requested. Consult Access Services for further information.
Boxes 151-155 (student records): Restricted until the year 2300.

acqinfo.head: Immediate Source of Acquisition
acqinfo.p: Purchased from James S. Jaffe Rare Books LLC on the James Marshall and Marie-Louise Osborn Collection Fund, 2023.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Organized into eight series: I. Writings, 1965-2019. II. Editorial Projects, 1992-2021. III. Professional Papers, 1980s-2021. IV. Correspondence, 1958-2020. V. Personal Papers, 1915-2021. VI. Printed Materials, 1978-2021. VII. Sound Recordings, 1973-2017. VIII. Digital Files.

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: Kwame Dawes is a Ghanaian-born author, editor, and educator. Born in Accra in 1962, Dawes spent most of the first decade of his life in Ghana before his family moved to Jamaica in 1971, where he attended Jamaica College and the University of the West Indies (B.A., English, 1983). Dawes taught in Jamaica for a couple years in the mid-1980s before moving to Fredericton, Canada to pursue graduate work at the University of New Brunswick (PhD, Comparative Literature, 1992). Since 1992, Dawes has lived in the U.S. and held teaching positions at the University of South Carolina, University of Nebraska-Lincoln, and Brown University. He is the author of numerous collections of poetry, critical studies, novels, a collection of short stories, and a memoir. His works include: Progeny of Air (Peepal Tree Press, 1994), Natural Mysticism: Towards a New Reggae Aesthetic in Caribbean Writing (Peepal Tree Press, 1999). New and Selected Poems, 1994-2002 (Peepal Tree Press, 2003), She's Gone (Akashic Books, 2007), A Far Cry from Plymouth Rock: A Personal Narrative (Peepal Tree Press, 2007), City of Bones (Northwestern University Press, 2017), and Sturge Town (Norton, 2024). Dawes has earned recognition both for his own literary output and for his work in support of other writers. He received the Forward Prize for Best First Poetry Collection for Progeny of Air in 1994 and a Windham-Campbell Prize for poetry in 2019. He is also co-founder (2001) and Artistic Director of the Calabash International Literary Festival and Poet Laureate of Jamaica (2024-2027).

prefercite.head: Preferred Citation
prefercite.p: Kwame Dawes Papers. James Weldon Johnson Collection, Yale Collection of American Literature, Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library, Yale University.

scopecontent.head: Content Description
scopecontent.p: The Kwame Dawes papers contain writings, professional papers, personal papers, printed materials, audiocassettes, and digitial files by or relating to Kwame Dawes. Collection materials document Dawes's activities as an author, editor, and educator. Specifically, collection materials document Dawes's interests in and work on the topics of Caribbean and reggae music and literature, African-American literature, race relations, and African literature and the literature coming out of the African diaspora. Materials addressing these and other topics can be found among his critical writings in Series I, the records of the African Poetry Book Fund (APBF) and other editorial projects in Series II, and course materials at the University of South Carolina in Series III.

userestrict.head: Conditions Governing Use
userestrict.p: The Kwame Dawes papers is the physical property of the Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library, Yale University. Literary rights, including copyright, belong to the authors or their legal heirs and assigns. For further information, consult the appropriate curator.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Series I is organized into three subseries: Writings of Kwame Dawes, Collaborations, and Writings of Others.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Series I includes writings by Kwame Dawes and others. The series is organized into three subseries for work by Dawes, collaborations between Dawes and others, and work by others.

Kwame Dawes's published work includes novels, academic studies, a personal narrative, plays, short stories, and poetry. The subseries for writings by Dawes is subdivided for book-length works across the genres, but there are also subdivisions for plays, arranged alphabetically by title, and other shorter works, such as addresses and lectures, book and project proposals, essays, interviews, reviews, short stories, and student papers. There are multiple drafts for most of Dawes's published fiction and academic work, including his novels She's Gone (Akashic Books, 2007) and Bivouac (Peepal Tree Press, 2010), and his study on the emergence of reggae in the 1960s and 1970s and its impact on Caribbean writing in Natural Mysticism: Towards a New Reggae Aesthetic (Peepal Tree Press, 1999). There are also multiple drafts of A Far Cry from Plymouth Rock: A Personal Narrative (Peepal Tree Press, 2007).

The bulk of Dawes's earliest writings can be found among the drafts of plays, many of which date between the early 1980s and early 1990s. Photographs in Series V. Personal Papers document stage productions of the plays "Charity's Come" and "Friends & Almost Lovers".

The Poetry subdivision consists of drafts and other materials for published collections, poetry manuscripts, and miscellaneous individual poems and foldered groups of poems. There are materials relating to 19 book-length poetry publications ranging from Dawes's first collection, Progeny of Air (Peepal Tree Press, 1994) to City of Bones (Northwestern University Press, 2017). Materials include drafts, often in the form of printouts, proofs, and, in some cases, publicity and reviews. Drafts of miscellaneous poems and untitled groups of poems, some with headings provided by Dawes, have been aggregated under the heading of "Poems."

Drafts of writings by Dawes can also be found among the notebooks filed in Series V. Personal Papers and among the digital files in Series VIII. Printed versions of work by Dawes, including editorials written for The Brunswickan , can be found among the clippings in Series VI. Printed Materials.

The subseries for Collaborations consists chiefly of published volumes of work by others to which Dawes added handwritten poems. There are 17 such titles, most of which contain reproductions of visual work by photographers or painters. Books are arranged alphabetically by the author or creator of the printed work. In the cases where Dawes added a collective title or heading to his own work inside the volume, the title is included in a folder note.

The subseries for Writings of Others aggregates single manuscripts and groups of writings by others. There is work by students, former students, and other writers either collected by or shared with Dawes--many manuscripts are inscribed to Dawes. The subseries also includes some manuscripts in submission format. The submissions filed here, often annotated and with comments by Dawes, are not clearly linked with any of the editorial projects represented in Series II. Manuscripts clearly linked with publishing projects on which Dawes served in an editorial capacity can be found in Series II. Editorial Projects.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Organized into five subdivisions: Non-fiction (Books); Novels, Novellas, and Short Story Collections; Plays; Poetry; and Shorter Works.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Includes typescript drafts and photocopies of individual plays by the following Caribbean playwrights: Michael Abbensetts, Douglas Archibald, Alwin Bully, Rawle Gibbons, Stafford Harrison, Ginger Knight, Victor Questel, and Dennis Scott

did.unittitle: Special issue of Callaloo on contemporary Caribbean poetry

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Series II is organized into fifteen subseries: African Poetry Book Fund, The Backwaters Press, A Bloom of Stones , National Black Theatre Festival Anthology, Obsidian III: Literature in the African Diaspora , Peepal Tree Press, Prairie Schooner Book Prize, Red: An Anthology of Contemporary Black British Poetry , So Much Things to Say , South Carolina Poetry Initiative, Spread the Word Anthology, The Sumter Essayist , Talk Yuh Talk: Interviews with Anglophone Caribbean Poets , "Twilight Stories from the Swamp Country," and Wheel and Come Again: An Anthology of Reggae Poetry .

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Series II consolidates materials relating to various publication projects on which Kwame Dawes has served in an editorial capacity.

The largest group of material in the series relates to the African Poetry Book Fund (APBF). The APBF was established by Dawes in 2012 in partnership with Prairie Schooner at the University of Nebraska and administers multiple book prizes and publications. APBF materials consist chiefly of annual submissions to the New-Generation African Poets chapbook series (2014-), but there are also submissions marked for the Sillerman First Book Prize for African Poetry and the Brunel International African Poetry Prize, as well as longer, book-length manuscripts for titles published in the African Poetry Book Series. Most of the New-Generation African Poets chapbooks are published in collaboration with Akashic Books. Submissions include brief biographical statements by the authors and handwritten annotations by Dawes, with ranking scores and comments and, in some cases, proofreaders' marks. For the earliest chapbook set, published in 2014 by Slapering Hol Press, there are both draft submissions and corrected page proofs.

In addition to the APBF materials, there are manuscript submissions to The Backwaters Press and Peepal Tree Press, submissions and drafts for several compilations, including Talk Yuh Talk: Interviews with Anglophone Caribbean Poets (2000), and submissions and other materials relating to two journals. In 1993 Dawes launched a journal of student writing, The Sumter Essayist , while on faculty at the University of South Carolina. Dawes also served on the editorial board of the journal Obsidian from 1996 through 2013, and in an advisory capacity since 2013. The subseries for Obsidian III is comprised of submissions and other material relating to a few issues of the journal. The bulk of the material relates to a special issue (Volume 2, Number 2 Fall/Winter 2000-2001) guest edited by Dawes on new Jamaican writers. Files for the special issue include submissions and page proofs.
Drafts or manuscript submissions not clearly linked with any of the many publication projects in Series II are filed with the Writings of Others in Series I. Writings.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Series III is organized into six subseries: Awards, Events and Programs, Subject Files, University of Nebraska--Lincoln, University of South Carolina, and Other.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Professional papers in Series III are subdivided for materials relating to appearances by Kwame Dawes at professional events and programs, subject files, materials relating to Dawes's faculty employments at the universities of South Carolina (1992-2011) and Nebraska-Lincoln (2012-2025), and assorted other material.

The Programs and Events subseries consists chiefly of mixed files relating to appearances by Dawes at various cultural and academic events. Files include correspondence, travel documents, itineraries and schedules, drafts of presentations, clippings, and other printed ephemera. Files relating to appearances by Dawes that contain printed materials exclusively can be found in Series VI.
The Subject Files subseries in Series III aggregates topical files containing research-oriented materials and notes relating, presumably, to book projects or courses taught by Dawes.

Assorted other materials include awards, copies of CVs, printouts from Dawes's personal website, and material relating to Ujamaa, the band Dawes performed with while a graduate student in Canada circa the late 1980s and early 1990s.

did.unittitle: Outstanding Research and Creative Activity Award, University of Nebraska--Lincoln

scopecontent.p: Includes photographs

did.unittitle: Proclamation by New York City Council

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Series IV is organized into two subseries: General Correspondence and Family Correspondence.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Series IV, subdivided for General and Family correspondence, contains the bulk of the correspondence in the Dawes papers. The General Correspondence subseries features files for authors, editors, educators, and academic, literary, and cultural institutions that document Dawes's professional activities. Correspondents include numerous Caribbean authors and educators of Caribbean literature, such as Cyril and David Dabydeen, Stewart Brown, Kendel Hippolyte, George Lamming, John Robert Lee, Faith Linton, Mervyn Morris, and Geoffrey Philp. There are also publishers with whom Dawes collaborated over a period of many years, such as Peepal Tress Press, and organizations with which Dawes has been active, such as Cave Canem. Finally, there is also correspondence from family, friends, and students. Groups of correspondence assembled by Dawes under the headings "Cards and postcards," "Correspondence about readings/conferences," and "Publishers" have been preserved. The Family Correspondence subseries contains a small amount of third party correspondence between family members.
Scattered files of correspondence can also be found for works and projects in Series I. Writings and Series II. Editorial Projects respectively.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Organized into five subdivisions: Helen Dawes Correspondence, Kojo Dawes Correspondence, Neville Dawes Correspondence, Sophie Dawes Correspondence, and Other.

scopecontent.p: Photocopies of correspondence from Dawes to Drayon originally dating from 1958 through 1982

scopecontent.p: Photocopies of letters between Dawes and Swanzy originally dating from 1951 through 1955

did.unittitle: College Board to Kekeli M. Dawes
Dawes, Gwyneth to Sena and Kekeli Dawes

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Series V is organized into five subseries: Family Papers, Financial and Legal Records, Notes and Notebooks, Photographs, and Other Material.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The Personal Papers in Series V includes family papers, financial and legal records, notebooks, photographs, and other material.

In addition to larger files of Notebooks and Photographs, Series V includes small quantities of family papers, financial records, such as publishing contracts, receipts, and royalty statements, and other material.

The Notebooks in Series V have been preserved together by format and arranged chronologically by date when known. The notebooks include handwritten course and reading notes, drafts of writings, and other content, such as biblical citations and discussion, sketches, lists, schedules, contact information, and directions. Notebooks range from an early "Class Room Library Note Book," which appears to have been marked at a later date as from 1973, "Jamaica College First Form," to a small volume with mixed content dating circa 2016. There are also four notebooks labelled as "original" drafts of City of Bones (Northwestern, 2017).

The Photographs subseries is subdivided for images of people, places, programs and events, stage and film productions, and negatives. There are photographs of Kwame Dawes alone and with others, including Wole Soyinka, Kamau Brathwaite, Otukwei Okai, and Elizabeth Alexander, as well as members of the reggae band, Ujamaa, Dawes performed with in the late 1980s. The subseries features circa 430 photographic prints produced while Dawes was in residence at the International Writing Program (IWP) at the University of Iowa. Dawes was one of 30 writers in residence over a period of approximately three months during the Fall of 1986. There are photographs of Dawes and fellow residents at locations in and around the university and Iowa City and on numerous excursions and trips. Fellow residents represented in photographs include the following: Luiz Berto, Bole Butake, Hugo Carrillo, Civacankari, Fidelito Cortes, Humberto Guzman, Inkeri Kilpinen, Leonard Koza, Marra Lanot, Femi Osofisan, and Edwin Thumboo, among others. Members of the University of Iowa community present in photographs include the co-founders of the IWP, Paul Engle and Hualing Nie (Engle), and Peter Nazareth. Other writers present in photographs but not identified as IWP residents include Joseph Brodsky, Robert Creeley, Michel Deguy, and Ngugi wa Thiong'o. Travel destinations include the home of Vachel Lindsay in Springfield, Illinois, the birthplace of Carl Sandburg in Galesburg, Illinois, the Mark Twain Riverboat on the Mississippi River in Hannibal, Missouri, the home ("Rowan Oak") of William Faulkner in Oxford, Mississippi, and the John Steinbeck House in Salinas, California.

scopecontent.p: Photographs include Dawes, Raymond Anderson, Roxanne Brown, and Donna Samuels

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Includes work entitled "Pale river" by Brian Rutenberg

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Series VI is organized into six subseries: Appearances by Kwame Dawes, Clippings, Periodicals, Promotional Material Relating to Works by Kwame Dawes, Publishers and Venues, and Other Material.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Material in Series VI is subdivided by content and format.
The subseries for Appearances by Kwame Dawes contains various forms of printed ephemera, such as cards, brochures, programs, and small broadsides, documenting readings, lectures, and other events.

The Clippings subseries features single pages or sheets and small sections of newspapers with content relating to Dawes and other matters. The group of Brunswickan clippings contain editorials and reviews written by Dawes between 1989 and 1991 while he was a student at the University of New Brunswick in Fredericton, Canada. The Periodicals feature entire issues of journals and newspapers and large newspaper sections. Content documents Dawes's literary and professional activities and interests. There are numerous papers from the communities around the universities of South Carolina and Nebraska--Lincoln.

The Promotional Materials Relating to Titles by Dawes and Publishers and Venues subseries contain ephemera relating either directly to works by Dawes or publishers, such as Peepal Tree Press, and institutions with which he was active. Other Materials include book jackets, brochures, business cards, catalogs, invitations, blank postcards, posters, and programs.

did.unittitle: Heart Free Hand, a Black Writers Series
Writer in residence at CCA, Glascow

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Includes photocopies of clippings

title.part: Calabash: A Newsletter for Writers of African and Asian Descent

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Includes "Memoirs of an Ex-playwright" by Dawes

did.unittitle: The Globe and Mail section D

title.part: Poetry News: The Newsletter of the Poetry Society

did.unittitle: The Post and Courier section B

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Includes "The Last Poem" by Dawes

did.unittitle: USA Today Black History Month 2019 special edition
Promotional Materials Relating to Titles by Kwame Dawes

title.part: Duppy Conqueror: New and Selected Poems
A Far Cry from Plymouth Rock
Wisteria: Twilight Songs from the Swamp Country

accessrestrict.head: Conditions Governing Access
accessrestrict.p: Boxes 132-137 (audiocassettes): Restricted fragile material. Reference copies may be requested. Consult Access Services for further information.
138 (record album storage): Restricted fragile material. Reference copies may be requested. Consult Access Services for further information.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Series VII is organized into two subseries: Audiocassettes and Other Formats.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Series VII consolidates analog audio recording formats. There are 66 non-commercial recordings on audiocassettes and one record album. Recordings include readings by Dawes and others, interviews with Dawes and others, and music. Titles from individual cassettes and cassette cases suggest that numerous tapes contain interviews, possibly relating to the editorial project Talk Yuh Talk: Interviews with Anglophone Caribbean Poets (University Press of Virignia, 2001).

The non-commercial cassette recordings are arranged alphabetically by heading, with headings taken from the cassette itelf, when present, and then the cassette case. Thirty-four commercial recordings are documented at the end of the alphabetical run.
Additional audio recordings in the collection, received as digital files, can be found in Series VIII. Digital Files.

did.unittitle: "David Dabydeen Int. [Caribbean Poets, Talk Yuh Talk], Miami"
"Interview with John Robert Lee by Kendel Hippolyte, 1"
"'The Legacy of a People' by K [?]"
"Lillian Allen Interview [for Talk Yuh Talk], tape #1"
"Marilyn Talks Back & Forth with the Sea by Dan Steven[son]"
"Mervyn Morris - [Caribbean Poets, Talk Yuh Talk interview] #3"
"SABC Radio, 'The Poet Speaks,' Sudeep Sen interviewed by Stephen Gray"

accessrestrict.head: Conditions Governing Access
accessrestrict.p: Box 151-155 (student records): Restricted until the year 3000.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Restricted materials consist chiefly of student records.
